summary: Dan Shapiro reflects on seven years of helping startups and distills his approach into actionable principles. He argues the best way to help the ecosystem is by building a great startup yourself, but for those giving advice, efficiency and directness matter most. He advocates for batch mentoring, prioritizing companies with traction, strict time management, and staying in your lane of expertise. The post culminates in a new policy: he'll ask startups seeking his advice to let him write about their situations for an O'Reilly book, turning individual help into collective wisdom.
key_points: The single greatest contribution to the startup ecosystem is building a successful startup yourself, which injects talent, capital, and optimism
Advice has the highest ROI when directed at startups already past early commitment hurdles — further down the funnel means less chance good advice gets wasted
Mentors should only advise in areas where they have genuine experience, not just opinions
Directness in short meetings beats politeness that wastes limited time
Shapiro introduces a 'pay it forward' policy: startups seeking his advice must agree to let their stories be shared to help others
money_quotes: I've always known that the greatest way to help startups is by building a great startup.
Success breeds success, injecting talent and cash in to the ecosystem.

The further down the funnel the startup is, the lower the chance that good advice will be rendered moot by one of the many random quirks that strike down entrepreneurs, sending them back to the clutches of Big Companies.
It's easy to have an opinion, but if there's no experience behind it, just explain that it's not an area of expertise and move on to a new topic.
Being direct is just a thin grey line away from being an asshole.
Why are you asking me how to marketing your social network when I've never done it before either? Let's focus on questions I'm actually qualified to answer for you.
I think the startup community suffers from a little too much secrecy.
key_insight: The most scalable way to help startups is to convert individual mentoring conversations into shared knowledge, turning private advice into a public good.
